fireResponse: Do not extinguish unless flow can be stopped. Cool surrounding containers with water. Eliminate ignition sources. Allow gas to burn if leak cannot be stopped.
notes: Common in Kigali households for cooking. Cylinders are present in most residential and commercial properties. Risk of BLEVE if cylinders are heated.

fireResponse: Do not use water directly on product. Use water spray to absorb vapors. Evacuate downwind area immediately. Notify water treatment authorities.
notes: Used in water treatment plants across Rwanda. Extremely toxic even at low concentrations. Yellow-green gas with pungent odor.

fireResponse: Use dry chemical, CO2, or foam. Do not use water jet. Cool nearby containers. Prevent runoff from entering drains or waterways.
notes: Very common at petrol stations throughout Rwanda. Highly flammable vapors can accumulate. Flash point approximately -43°C.

fireResponse: Use dry chemical, CO2, foam, or sand. Water can be used to cool containers. Contain spill to prevent waterway contamination.
notes: Widely used in vehicles and generators across Rwanda. Less volatile than petrol. Environmental contamination risk is high.

fireResponse: Use dry chemical or CO2. Foam may be used. Ventilate area. Prevent vapors from accumulating in low-lying areas.
notes: Found in hardware stores and construction sites. Can produce toxic fumes when burned. Multiple products may be stored together.

fireResponse: Do not use water directly — generates heat. Use dry sand or dry chemical. Neutralize with sodium bicarbonate. Prevent runoff contamination.
notes: Found in car batteries, industrial facilities. Highly corrosive. Water contact produces violent reaction and toxic fumes. GBV weapon of choice in some cases.

fireResponse: May explode if confined. Use large quantities of water to absorb vapors. Do not extinguish unless flow can be stopped. Evacuate downwind.
notes: Used in refrigeration systems and agricultural fertilizers. Strong pungent odor. Flammable in high concentrations. Toxic inhalation hazard.

fireResponse: Use large amounts of water. Decomposes to release oxygen which intensifies fires. Keep away from combustibles. Do not use dry chemical.
notes: Used in medical/pharmaceutical settings and hair salons. High concentrations are explosive. Reacts with organic material.

fireResponse: Not flammable. Use water to flush spills. Neutralize with dilute acid. Prevent runoff. Generates heat when dissolved in water.
notes: Used in soap making, food processing, and industrial cleaning in Rwanda. Causes severe chemical burns. Solid pellets or concentrated solutions.

fireResponse: Do not extinguish unless flow can be stopped. Cool cylinders with water. Extremely shock-sensitive — handle gently. Risk of spontaneous explosion.
notes: Used in welding and cutting operations at workshops across Kigali. Highly unstable at pressures above 103 kPa. Must be stored upright.

fireResponse: Use alcohol-resistant foam or CO2. Water may be ineffective. Methanol burns with an invisible flame. Ventilate area thoroughly.
notes: Used as industrial solvent and fuel additive. Toxic if ingested — causes blindness and death. Sometimes illegally added to illicit alcohol.

fireResponse: Use alcohol-resistant foam, CO2, or dry chemical. Water can dilute but may spread fire. Keep containers cool with water spray.
notes: Common in medical facilities, breweries, and local distilleries in Rwanda. Highly flammable. Flash point 13°C.

fireResponse: Do not extinguish burning oxygen. Remove cylinders from fire area if safe. Cool with water. Oxygen dramatically intensifies all fires.
notes: Used in hospitals, clinics, and welding. Compressed cylinders pose explosion risk if heated. Oil and oxygen contact is extremely dangerous.

fireResponse: Use water fog, foam, or CO2. Avoid breathing smoke — produces toxic gases. Contain runoff to prevent soil and water contamination. Notify agricultural authorities.
notes: Widely used in Rwandan agriculture (tea, coffee, rice cultivation). Products vary widely in toxicity. Organophosphate pesticides are particularly hazardous.

fireResponse: Not flammable but battery cases may burn. Use water to flush spills. Neutralize with baking soda. Lead contamination from battery plates.
notes: Found in vehicles and motorcycle batteries throughout Rwanda. Dilute sulfuric acid (~30%). Contact with skin causes burns. Lead contamination hazard.
